summarization.a: The Co 2p XPS spectrum of the PE-ALD Co film on Si(001) shows well-defined Co 2p₃⁄₂ and Co 2p₁⁄₂ peaks, indicating the presence of metallic cobalt with no pronounced satellite features associated with cobalt oxides.
summarization.b: The O 1s spectrum exhibits a very weak and broad signal near 530–532 eV, suggesting minimal oxygen incorporation and confirming low oxide contamination in the deposited Co film.
summarization.c: The N 1s spectrum shows no distinct peak above the noise level, indicating negligible nitrogen incorporation from the plasma during the PE-ALD process.
summarization.d: The C 1s spectrum displays only a very low-intensity background signal, demonstrating minimal carbon contamination and high chemical purity of the cobalt film.

a.question: Does plasma-enhanced ALD using NH₃ plasma effectively remove precursor ligands during cobalt film growth?

b.question: Compared to thermal ALD, how does the impurity level (C, O, N) change in PE-ALD Co films?
b.answer: Impurity levels are drastically reduced and approach near-zero.

d.question: Why is the chemical purity demonstrated in this figure critical for cobalt interconnect applications?
d.answer: High-purity metallic cobalt is essential for achieving low electrical resistivity and reliable performance in interconnect structures. The XPS spectra show negligible carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen incorporation, confirming effective ligand removal by NH₃ plasma. This purity directly enables Co films suitable for nanoscale device integration, unlike carbon-rich films formed by thermal ALD.

c.question: Which spectral features indicate the formation of metallic cobalt in the PE-ALD film?
c.answer: Sharp Co 2p₃/₂ peak at ~778 eV, Clear Co 2p₁/₂ peak at ~793 eV, Absence of strong satellite peaks, Near-baseline O 1s and C 1s signals
